fact: Every day in the US, about 2,000 workers suffer job-related eye injuries that require medical treatment.
Eye injuries cost more than $300 million per year in lost productivity, medical treatment, and workers' compensation.
About 10-20% of all work-related eye injuries cause temporary or permanent vision loss.
source: National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH)

fact: The majority of eye injuries occur because workers are not wearing any eye protection at the time of injury, or are wearing the wrong kind for the job.
Chemical burns account for 7-10% of all ocular trauma and represent some of the most devastating injuries to the eye.
Alkaline chemical burns (from chemicals like NaOH) are more dangerous than acid burns because they penetrate deeper into eye tissue and continue to damage even after irrigation begins.
Workers 25 years old and younger are disproportionately affected by occupational injuries — they are twice as likely to be injured as workers over 45.
source: NIOSH Young Worker Safety and Health

fact: Laboratory accidents result in approximately 500,000 injuries per year globally, with chemical exposure being the most common cause.
source: World Health Organization (WHO) Occupational Health Report

fact: The average cost of a lost-time hand injury (e.g., from a chemical burn) is $7,700 in direct medical costs, not including lost productivity.
In the UK, the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) reports that over 12,000 workers per year develop occupational skin disease — the most common of which is contact dermatitis from chemical exposure.
Fume hoods, when used correctly, can reduce chemical vapour exposure by more than 1,000-fold compared to working in open air.
Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) is responsible for more industrial fatalities than any other chemical except carbon monoxide. It can incapacitate at 100 ppm and kill within minutes at 500 ppm.
Only 16% of workers use a fire extinguisher correctly without prior training. The PASS technique (Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep) is the international standard.
Laboratory fires caused by improperly stored or handled flammable solvents account for over 1,000 incidents per year in US academic institutions alone.
Thermal burns from hot liquids and steam are the most frequent injury in biology and chemistry labs, responsible for 31% of all lab-related injuries.
The time to permanent eye damage from a corrosive chemical splash can be as little as 3–5 seconds. The eyewash station is only effective if reached within 10 seconds.
source: ANSI Z358.1 Emergency Eyewash and Shower Standard

fact: Benzene exposure — even at levels considered 'safe' — is associated with a 40% increased risk of leukemia. Many lab chemists were exposed to benzene for decades before it was understood.
source: International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC)

fact: Studies show that PPE compliance in academic laboratories is significantly lower than in industrial settings. One survey found fewer than 40% of students consistently wore goggles during lab work.
Glove selection is critical: latex gloves offer essentially no protection against many organic solvents, which can permeate through in under 1 minute. Always check the chemical compatibility chart.
The annual economic cost of occupational injuries and illnesses in the United States exceeds $250 billion — more than the combined cost of all cancers.
source: Journal of Occupational and Environmental Medicine
